title: Graph Valid Tree
problem_statement: You have a graph of n nodes labeled from 0 to n - 1. You are given an integer n and a list of edges where edges[i] = [ai, bi] indicates that there is an undirected edge between ai and bi in the graph. Return true if the edges of the given graph make up a valid tree, and false otherwise.

examples.explanation: The graph is a valid tree.
The graph is not a valid tree because there is a cycle between nodes 1, 2, and 3.

constraints: There are no self-loops or repeated edges.
intuition: A tree is an undirected graph that is connected and acyclic. It must have exactly n-1 edges and no cycles.

approaches.name: Union-Find
approaches.description: Check if edges == n-1, then use Union-Find to detect cycles. If no cycles and all connected, it's a tree.
approaches.why_not: DFS/BFS also work, but Union-Find is efficient for this.
interview_commentary: First check edges count: must be n-1 for a tree.
Union-Find detects cycles: if two nodes already in same component, cycle.
Since we checked edges, if no cycles, it's connected.

approaches.name: DFS for Connectivity and Cycles
approaches.description: Build graph, DFS from node 0, count visited nodes and detect cycles using parent tracking.
approaches.why_not: Union-Find is simpler, but DFS works for small n.
interview_commentary: DFS checks for cycles by avoiding parent and checking visited.
Also ensures all nodes visited for connectivity.
Edge count check prevents disconnected components.

interview_qa.a: Tree has no cycles, so minimum edges for connectivity.

interview_qa.q: Can a graph have n-1 edges but not be connected?
interview_qa.a: No, because with n-1 edges, if no cycles, it must be connected.
Tree is connected forest; forest has multiple components.
Union-Find is iterative and efficient for large n.
